pages.title: About Me
pages.text: Hey, I’m Johann :)Welcome to the blog - this is where I discuss things going on in my life and whatever it is I’m working on. I wrote this blog in two days mainly for portfolio reasons, what with university applications and it’s a good way to link all my stuff together, but it also coincided with a desire to write about my experiences and get my insight out into the world. It’s my two cents against everyone else’s, but hey, you could buy two things worth a cent with that! ProgrammingIt’s my thing, ok? My first taste of programming was when I was about 10 years old, believe it or not it was Warwick Davis doing some programme for channel 4 where he taught me and a bunch of other kids how to use Scratch (I’ve moved on, don’t panic). It was years later when I was really bored in a French lesson when I started to think about how I could find prime numbers and if I could automate it into a program. Long story short, I have experience in C++, C#, Python, JavaScript, and x86 Assembly. I’ve written some basic programming langauges, a very basic kernel, and even a balanced quinary emulator for my EPQ. MusicI am a music man and will always be. I play guitar, bass, ukulele, and can do some drumming (but I’m pretty awful at it). My school has given me the oppourtunity to play in multiple bands mostly covering contemporary rock, pop, and jazz as a bassist, but I’ve played math rock as a guitarist on my own for a few years, and have occasionally taken part in gigs. I also try to write my own music, but it’s not that straightforward all the time. :/ /etc/I find it hard to write extensively about myself, it just feels incoherent, so here are a laod of things about me: Born, raised, and educated in Yorkshire. I’ve been rowing since I was eleven, and I’ve competed for my school in Durham, Stockton-upon-Tees, York, Eton Dorney, and the Netherlands. Proud member of team Oxford Comma (it’s a, b, and c) Fan of both Star Wars and Star Trek. I speak German proficiently, but have experience in French, Latin, Russian, and Klingon. I use arch btw

posts.title: install-arch
posts.text: Arch Linux Installation TutorialUntil about 6 months ago, I’d never really considered linux to be an operating system I’d use in the future - Windows was familiar to me, I knew how to make programs work in it, and linux just seemed to difficult to get working.But eventually I chose to bit the proverbial bullet and switched my PC over from Windows 11 to Garuda, and I was immediately hooked. I later switched to Arch on that computer, and having recently fixed an old laptop of mine I’ve installed it on that too. The one thing that did get me though was that I was following two tutorials, one from the official Arch wiki and another from phoenixNAP, with a variety of other websites to set up the rest of my operating system.So, I decided to inaugurate my blog with a tutorial on how I installed and set up Arch, let’s go! My desktop on Arch My SetupThe great thing about Arch is that you get a very minimal setup upon install - a command line is pretty much all you get. On the one hand, it can make it somewhat daunting to setup, but on the other hand, you can customise it pretty much however you want. I’ll be showing you how to install arch and set it up like mine, but you can totally change some things around to suit your preferences! If you’re a first time linux user, I suggest trying out a less intensive distro first - Garuda is quite good for gaming on Linux, but if you’re not so bothered about that, try out Manjaro or Ubuntu. One thing to note however is that Ubuntu is based on Debian, but Garuda and Manjaro are based on Arch, so they’re a little different. What you’ll needArch install mediaYou can download a .iso of arch linux from the official website and burn it onto a CD or USB. The tools I’ve used for this are Rufus on Windows, GNOME Disk Utility for Linux GNOME users, and Popsicle for all Linux users. An Internet ConnectionWhile it is possible to configure WiFi networks in the Arch Install Media, I’ve never done it. I opt to use an ethernet cable plugged into the back of my PC or Laptop - the live environment should have all the correct drivers installed so you don’t need to worry much about getting onto the internet. BackupsInstalling Arch on a drive will destroy all data already on that drive. As such, it’s very important to either make a backup of your drive or at least make a copy of anything you want off the drive (think pictures, songs, documents, configurations, etc.). Installing ArchN.B. I’ll be doing this in QEMU, so the Machine and View windows you see at the top won’t be there for you. You should now be ready to install Arch on your computer. Turn your computer off, plug in your install media, turn your computer on, and enter the BIOS to change the boot order. Accessing BIOS varies between motherboard manufacturers, but typically you’ll want to press either Del or one of the F1-F12 keys - check your motherboard manual or their website for more information on how to do this. Once in the BIOS, change the boot order to boot from USB or Diskette drive first (whichever your install media is on). You’ll be greeted by a menu providing different options, press enter with Arch Linux Install Medium selected. Boot options for the install media. Yours might look different to this if you booted in UEFI mode. After a while, you’ll be greeted with a shell. This is where we’re going to be inputting a slew of commands, so get ready for some typing. Be careful though, misclicks could be a serious problem. The arch shell you’ll be greeted with after loading. Pre-install checksFirst comes the simple but important step to make your life way easier - loading up the locale. Because keyboards differ between languages and regions, you might find that trying to type in one character comes out as a completely different character on the screen. I remedy this by running: loadkeys uk If you want a different locale for a different keyboard, try running ls /usr/share/kbd/keymaps/**/*.map.gz and pick out the locale name you want, then run the loadkeys command with your choice. Since you need an internet connection, it’s a good idea to check it’s present before you’ve done lots of work. Run ping somecollagist.github.io and check for responses. If this fails, check your ethernet cables and internet connection. I suggest checking to see if your system supports UEFI booting. UEFI is a more secure way of booting operating systems which is included on most modern hardware. Keep in mind that UEFI-enabled systems are still capable of booting operating systems in BIOS mode, which makes this a good choice for security. This article will outline how to install arch on both UEFI drives and BIOS drives. You can check how your computer booted your install medium by running ls /sys/firmware/efi/efivars and if there are no errors reported, you’re in UEFI mode. Finally, check that your system clock is correct. Do this by running timedatectl set-ntp true && timedatectl status and if it’s the correct time (it’s in UTC remember), you’re all good to go. Preparing the drivesHere comes the scary bit. We need to install arch on our drive, which will likely require the formatting and wiping of the entire disk - this isn’t strictly necessary but I would suggest it as dualbooting is beyond the scope of this article. This article will assume you will wipe your drive entirely. Let’s start by listing the disks present. Run fdisk -l to see which storage devices are currently connected to the computer. Identify which drive you want to install Arch on (probably by it’s storage size) - it’ll probably be called something like /dev/sda or /dev/nvme0n1. For the purposes of demonstration, this article will say you want to install Arch on /dev/sda. fdisk -l Setting up drives for BIOSOn BIOS, you want to create a DOS partition table for your drive. Run fdisk /dev/sda and press o (this creates a DOS partition table), enter, then w to write this to the disk. Now, run cfdisk /dev/sda to create the disk partitions. We only need one partition on BIOS, but most users allocate a Linux Swap partition, which is where virtual memory will be stored. The typical partition layout for a BIOS drive is as follows: Type Size What we’ll call it Linux Swap At least 512MiB [ROOTPART] Linux The rest of your device [SWAP] Setting up drives for UEFIOn UEFI, you want to create a GPT partition table for your drive. Run fdisk /dev/sda and press g (this creates a GPT partition table), enter, then w to write this to the disk. Now, run cfdisk /dev/sda to create the disk partitions. We only need two partition on GPT, but most users allocate a Linux Swap partition, which is where virtual memory will be stored. The typical partition layout for a UEFI drive is as follows: Type Size What we’ll call it EFI System At least 300 MiB [EFIPART] Linux Swap At least 512MiB [ROOTPART] Linux The rest of your device [SWAP] An example of creating filesystems on your drive, I’m using a DOS partition table. Creating partitions in cfdisk Create a new partition by selecting the new tab at the bottom with the arrow keys. Press enter and input a size for your drive. Press enter, select the primary option, and press enter again. With the partition selected, select the type tab at the bottom with the arrow keys. Press enter, select which partition type you need, and press enter again. Repeat for all the other partitions you need, ensuring you select free space for new partitions. If you’re using a BIOS drive, for your root partiton [ROOTPART], make sure you select the bootable tab and press enter. Write your partitions to the drive, this cannot be undone! Type ‘yes’ to confirm this. Keep note of the device names, such as /dev/sda1 and what partitions they correspond to - whenever I write [ROOTPART], you should write the corresponding device name. You can then quit the cfdisk dialog. Assigning filesystemsOnce you’ve created the partitions for your drive, you need to give them filesystems in order to actually work. If you created an EFI partition, runYours might look different to this if you booted in UEFI mode. For your Linux partition, run mkfs.ext4 [ROOTPART] to create an ETX4 partition. Keep in mind that if you would rather use a different format, like BTRFS, you should run the relevant command instead. This should be done now. An example of creating filesystems on your partitions Mounting drivesNow we need to ‘mount’ our drives to actually use them and install Arch onto them. This is simple enough; run the corresponding commands for whichever partitions you’ve made: Partition Name Command Linux mount [ROOTPART] /mnt EFI System mount --mkdir [EFIPART] /mnt/boot Linux Swap swapon [SWAP] Mount the drives Getting the reflectorArch uses pacman to install packages on your arch system, and it uses mirrors (websites which point to databases of packages) in order to do this. Since Arch can be installed this way, it is imperative to set up the reflector now so packages can be collected for installation. The reflector on the install media will be copied over into the final system during installation. To get started, run pacman -Syy && pacman -S reflector to sync the pacman databases and install the reflector package. Install the reflector package Once this is done, run cp /etc/pacman.d/mirrorlist /etc/pacman.d/mirrorlist.bak to create a backup of the current mirrorlist. The install media does come with a list of mirrors, but it’s best to update it. The above command creates a copy of the current mirrorlist so if something goes wrong, you can restore it. Once you’re ready to update the mirror list, run reflector -c "XX" -f 12 -l 10 -n 12 --save /etc/pacman.d/mirrorlist is a country code - for me that's GB. to get a list of country codes, run123456789101112131415161718192021222324252627282930313233343536373839404142434445464748495051525354555657585960616263646566676869707172737475767778798081828384858687888990919293949596979899```reflector --list-countries```and use the corresponding country code.## Install ArchIt's time. To install Arch on a the drive, run``` pacstrap /mnt base linux-firmware sudo dhcpcd [text-editor] [kernel]```**but replace [text-editor] and [kernel] with packages of your choice.** For my text editor, I use ```nano```, and the most popular kernel packages are ```linux``` (the basic model) or ```linux-zen``` (good for gaming). Once you've written this command with a text editor and a kernel included, hit enter and let the install media do it's thing. This takes some time, so be patient.|![Run pacstrap to install Arch](install-arch/pacstrap.png "Run pacstrap to install Arch")||---||Run ```pacstrap``` to install Arch||![Ideal output of pacstrap](install-arch/pacstrap_done.png "Ideal output of pacstrap")||---||Ideal output of ```pacstrap```|Once this is done, type the command```genfstab -U /mnt >> /mnt/etc/fstab```This is very important, as this will define which drives should be mounted at startup, including the drive where your operating system is stored.***## Getting into the installed systemYou should now be at a point where you can start configuring your system - in order to do this you must first run this command:```arch-chroot /mnt```Immediately after running this, you may notice that your shell has changed. Instead of using ZSH (the default terminal for arch install media), you are now using Bash. This is because Bash is the default shell for an *installed* Arch system. Any commands you run now will be performed directly on your root partition [ROOTPART].|![arch-chroot /mnt](install-arch/chroot.png "arch-chroot /mnt")||---||arch-chroot /mnt means that your shell's root directory is now on your mounted drive, not the install media.|## Setting up the local informationI usually set the clock first before I customise anything. Run```timedatectl list-timezones```to list all available timezones. They'll be separated into Region/City, so look for your continent and a nearby city. For me, my timezone is Europe/London, so I run the command```timedatectl set-timezone Europe/London```For the next few steps, I will be modifying text in files with my text editor, nano, but if you've chosen to use a different editor that's totally ok. This tutorial will assume you're using nano.Now I suggest setting the locale. This will (hopefully) make your keyboard layout permanent, and is equivalent to setting your computer language in other operating systems. You need to edit the file ```/etc/locale.gen```, so I would run```nano /etc/locale.gen```Once I run this, I'll be presented with a text file will look a little like this:![Contents of /etc/locale.gen](install-arch/localegen.png "Contents of /etc/locale.gen")All those hash characters (#) on the sides are comment characters. They tell the system to ignore any characters after them on a line, so currently there's no locale set. Scroll down to your locale (it'll usually be two lowercase characters for your language and two uppercase characters for your region, mine is en_GB), and remove the hash character before it. Make sure you do this to the one with UTF-8. (See the above picture.) If you aren't sure which locale you should go with, look up which one is right for you. Make a note of your locale, and in nano, press ```Ctrl+S``` (to save) and then ```Ctrl+X``` (to exit)Now we should edit the ```/etc/locale.conf``` file; I will run```nano /etc/locale.conf```type in the following```LANG=[locale_name]```and then save and exit this file.|![Editing locale.conf](install-arch/localeconf.png "Editing locale.conf")||---||How I set up locale.conf|To finish setting up the locale, run```locale-gen```## Setting up internet accessAccess to the internet is pretty crucial, so let's sort that out now. To get started, run```nano /etc/hostname```and write in a name for your computer. Remember this, we'll need it in a bit.|![Pick a hostname](install-arch/hostname.png "Pick a hostname")||---||Pick a hostname|Now run```nano /etc/hosts```and type the following, replacing MyDevice with whatever you've called your device: 127.0.0.1 localhost::1 localhost127.0.1.1 MyDevice 1234567891011121314151617181920212223242526272829303132333435363738394041424344454647484950515253545556575859|![Set up host aliases](install-arch/hosts.png "Set up hsot aliases")||---||Set up host aliases|And to finish setting up internet access, run```systemctl enable --now dhcpcd```This will activate ```dhcpcd.service``` immediately and on startup, so you shouldn't need to mess about with the internet much more.|![Enable the DHCPCD service](install-arch/dhcpcd.png "Enable the DHCPCD service")||---||Enable the DHCPCD service - I got a weird error here but it seems fine|## Adding a userUp to this point, you've been configuring the device as the root user. For those people coming from Windows or MacOS, the root user is Linux's equivalent of an administrator. This should be secure, so set a root password by running ```passwd```|![Setting the root password](install-arch/rootpasswd.png "Setting the root password")||---||Setting the root password - you won't be able to see what you type|It's worth creating a seperate user so you have a home directory but don't worry, you can easily be granted administrator priviledges (or root access if you want to sound fancy). To start, run the command```useradd -m -G wheel -s /bin/bash username```|![Adding a user](install-arch/makeuser.png "Adding a user")||---||Adding a user|You can change username to whatever you like. The ```-m``` option gives you a home directory located at ```/home/username``` (though you may see it often written as ```~```), ```-G wheel``` adds you to the wheel group (more on that later), and ```-s /bin/bash``` sets your default shell to Bash (you can easily change Bash out for another terminal like Fish or ZSH). You should then set a password by running```passwd username```To get root access, you must modify the ```sudoers``` file. This can be done by running the command```export EDITOR=nano & visudo```and decommenting the line ```%wheel ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL```|![Setting up sudo access](install-arch/sudoers.png "Setting up sudo access")||---||Setting up sudo access|## Installing the bootloaderThis step is crucial - the bootloader is a program run when your computer turns on which is responsible for loading the operating system. Most linux users use GRUB to perform this task, but it differs between if your drive boots in BIOS or UEFI mode. This article assumes your computer uses an x86 or x64 architecture - if you use a different processor type, this article is probably not something you need!|![Installing GRUB on a BIOS drive](install-arch/grub.png "Installing GRUB on a BIOS drive")||---||Installing GRUB on a BIOS drive|### GRUB for BIOSRun the following commands to install GRUB for BIOS drives pacman -S grub os-probergrub-install –target=i386-pc /dev/sdagrub-mkconfig -o /boot/grub/grub.cfg 12345### GRUB for UEFIRun the following commands to install grub for UEFI drives pacman -S grub efibootmgrmount –mkdir [EFIPART] /boot/efigrub-install –target=x86_64-efi –bootloaderid=GRUB –efi-directory=/boot/efigrub-mkconfig -o /boot/grub/grub.cfg ``` Give it a spinHopefully you’re all ready to go with Arch! Type exit and then reboot. Don’t forget to go into your BIOS when rebooting and ensure you’re now booting from your drive and not the install media.

posts.title: Starting blog...
posts.text: Hey! My name’s Johann, and this is obviously my blog. Initial commit and all that, maybe go to the about me section, it’s kinda self explanatory hehe. Cool sweet, Johann.
